summarization.a: The figure presents real-time ellipsometry trajectories (Δ vs Ψ) comparing cyclic ALE with continuous Ar/C₄F₈ plasma etching during processing of a SiO₂–Si–SiO₂ multilayer stack. The cyclic ALE trajectory exhibits discrete, stepwise movement corresponding to controlled layer-by-layer processing, whereas continuous etching shows a smoother, continuous trajectory, indicating different surface reaction pathways and selectivity between top SiO₂, Si, and bottom SiO₂ layers.

d.question: Why is trajectory-based ellipsometry analysis important for evaluating ALE performance?
d.answer: Ellipsometry trajectory analysis allows direct, real-time differentiation between controlled cyclic ALE and uncontrolled continuous etching. This enables identification of layer transitions, verification of self-limiting behavior, and optimization of etch selectivity in complex multilayer stacks.

b.question: How do the ellipsometry trajectories differ between cyclic ALE and continuous Ar/C₄F₈ etching?
b.answer: Cyclic ALE shows stepwise, segmented trajectories, whereas continuous etching produces a smooth, continuous trajector

a.question: Does the figure use real-time ellipsometry to distinguish cyclic ALE from continuous plasma etching?

c.question: Which surface or layer-resolved properties are reflected by the Δ–Ψ trajectory shape?
c.answer: Selective etching of top SiO₂, Transition to Si etching, Bottom SiO₂ exposure, Layer-by-layer removal behavior
